title: Present Bias Is Why You Keep Paying Your Worst Credit Card Last

notes: Most personal finance advice says pay the highest-interest debt first (the avalanche method) — but most people don't, and that's not a discipline failure, it's present bias in action. The psychological cost of a problem feels smaller when it's further away, so the $400 minimum-payment card with 24% APR gets ignored while the $2,000 balance with 14% APR gets attacked. Use Maya's freelance cash-flow reality as the grounding scenario: irregular income makes present bias worse because every payment feels like a present-tense sacrifice. Close with one reframe: structuring payments as a commitment device rather than a monthly decision reduces the bias's grip.

title: The Psychology Behind Why You Avoid Checking Your Credit Score (And Why Checking It Actually Helps)
secondary_keywords: financial avoidance behavior|credit score anxiety|ostrich effect personal finance|progress monitoring credit building
notes: Ground this in the 'ostrich effect' — the documented behavioral tendency to avoid information we expect to be negative, even when knowing it would help us act. Research shows people check financial accounts less frequently during periods of perceived financial stress, compounding the problem. The counter-argument: visibility drives progress. Studies on goal monitoring show that people who track a measurable target consistently outperform those who don't. Frame credit monitoring not as a report card but as a feedback loop — and show Dominique, who is already doing everything right, exactly what she gains by watching the numbers move.

title: Present Bias Is Why You Keep Choosing the Wrong Loan — Here's How to Outsmart It
secondary_keywords: psychology of borrowing decisions|why people choose high interest loans|behavioral economics personal finance|how to make better money decisions
notes: Present bias — the tendency to overweight immediate relief against future cost — explains why a 24% APR offer feels acceptable when the alternative is a delayed closing or a missed opportunity. Dominique has the income and the score to do better; she just needs a framework that reframes the decision window. Anchor with research showing that naming a future cost in concrete dollars (not percentages) reduces present-bias-driven choices by up to 40%.
